nodes.name: Nobel Prizes in Science
nodes.description: Notable Nobel laureates in Physics, Chemistry, and Medicine

items.prompt: Which German physicist won the very first Nobel Prize in Physics in 1901 for discovering a new penetrating radiation he called 'X' because its nature was unknown?
items.explanation:
Discovered X-rays on November 8, 1895, in Würzburg, Germany; first Nobel Prize in Physics (1901)
His first famous image: his wife Anna Bertha's hand showing her bones and wedding ring — she reportedly said 'I have seen my own death'
X-rays (called Röntgen radiation in German) revolutionized medicine; physicians began using them within weeks of the announcement
Refused to patent his discovery, insisting it should benefit humanity freely
Died in poverty in 1923 after post-WWI hyperinflation destroyed his savings

items.prompt: Which Russian physiologist won the 1904 Nobel Prize in Medicine for research on digestion, and became more famous for experiments showing dogs could learn to salivate at the sound of a bell?
items.explanation:
Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine 1904 for research on the physiology of digestive glands — the first Russian to win a Nobel
Famous dog experiments: dogs salivated at a bell previously associated with food, demonstrating classical (Pavlovian) conditioning
An unconditioned reflex (food → saliva) can become a conditioned reflex (bell → saliva) through learned association
Continued research under the Bolshevik government; publicly criticized Stalin despite being protected from arrest
'Pavlovian' is now a common adjective for any automatic stimulus-response learned behavior

items.prompt: Which New Zealand-born physicist won the 1908 Nobel Prize in Chemistry — not Physics — for discovering that radioactive decay transmutes elements, and later proposed the nuclear model of the atom?
items.explanation:
Born 1871 in Nelson, New Zealand; died 1937 in Cambridge, England
Nobel Prize in Chemistry 1908 (he quipped being transformed from a physicist to a chemist overnight)
Gold foil experiment (Geiger-Marsden, 1909–1911): proved atoms have a tiny, dense, positive nucleus — overturning Thomson's 'plum pudding' model
First to artificially split the atom (1917): bombarded nitrogen with alpha particles to produce oxygen and hydrogen
Called the 'father of nuclear physics'; element 104 (rutherfordium, Rf) is named for him
items.cardFront: Chemistry, 1908 — nuclear model of the atom

items.prompt: Which Canadian physician won the 1923 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine for discovering insulin as a treatment for diabetes — and was so outraged by who shared the prize that he split his half with the excluded co-discoverer?
items.explanation:
Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine 1923, shared with lab director John Macleod
Discovered insulin with medical student Charles Best in 1921 — Banting was furious Best was excluded; he publicly split his prize money with Best
First insulin injection in a diabetic: 14-year-old Leonard Thompson, January 11, 1922 — the patient's condition improved dramatically
Macleod was in Scotland for much of the key experimental work; the prize allocation became one of the most controversial in Nobel history
Killed in a plane crash over Newfoundland in 1941 at age 49, on a secret WWII scientific mission to Britain

items.prompt: Which American chemist is the only person to have won two unshared Nobel Prizes in different categories — Chemistry in 1954 and Peace in 1962?
items.explanation:
Chemistry Nobel 1954 for research on chemical bonding: hybridization, resonance, the electronegativity scale, and protein secondary structure
Peace Nobel 1962 for circulating a petition against nuclear weapons testing signed by over 11,000 scientists
Only person to win two unshared Nobels in different categories; Marie Curie won two in different sciences but the Peace category is different
Narrowly missed a third Nobel: proposed the alpha helix structure of proteins, but Watson and Crick beat him to the DNA double helix
Championed megadose Vitamin C in his later years; the scientific consensus largely rejected his claims

items.prompt: Which American geneticist won the 1983 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine — awarded 32 years after her key research — for discovering that genes can move between chromosomes, a phenomenon she called transposons?
items.explanation:
Born 1902 in Hartford, CT; died 1992 in Cold Spring Harbor, NY, at age 90
Discovered transposable elements ('jumping genes') in maize (corn) in the late 1940s and early 1950s
Her work was largely dismissed or ignored for decades until the molecular biology revolution of the 1970s proved her correct
Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine 1983 — at 81, one of the oldest first-time laureates; the only woman to win an unshared Nobel in that category
Transposons now known to make up ~45% of the human genome; central to understanding cancer, evolution, and genome regulation

items.prompt: Which American biochemist won the 1993 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for inventing PCR (polymerase chain reaction), a technique for amplifying tiny DNA samples that revolutionized medicine, forensics, and molecular biology?
items.explanation:
Conceived PCR in 1983 while driving along a California highway; colleagues were initially skeptical
PCR amplifies a tiny DNA sample millions of times in a few hours — enabling DNA fingerprinting, pathogen detection (including COVID-19 tests), ancient DNA analysis, and cancer diagnosis
Nobel Prize in Chemistry 1993, shared with Michael Smith
Notoriously unconventional: wrote about personal encounters with extraterrestrials; his memoir *Dancing Naked in the Mind Field* documented his unorthodox views
Died in August 2019; his invention became globally indispensable just months later during the COVID-19 pandemic

items.prompt: Which Australian physician won the 2005 Nobel Prize in Medicine for proving that stomach ulcers are caused by bacterial infection — famously demonstrating his theory by drinking a solution of the bacterium himself?
items.explanation:
Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine 2005, shared with Robin Warren, for discovering *Helicobacter pylori* as the cause of gastric ulcers and gastritis
The medical establishment long insisted ulcers were caused by stress and excess acid; Marshall and Warren faced years of rejection
In 1984, Marshall drank a Petri dish culture of *H. pylori*, developed gastritis, then cured himself with antibiotics — one of the most dramatic self-experiments in medical history
The discovery overturned decades of treatment: most ulcers are now curable with a 1–2 week antibiotic course
*H. pylori* is also a Class I carcinogen; eradicating it reduces stomach cancer risk

items.prompt: Which Chinese pharmacologist won the 2015 Nobel Prize in Medicine for discovering artemisinin as an effective malaria treatment — the first Chinese woman and first mainland Chinese citizen to win a Nobel Prize in science?
items.explanation:
Born 1930 in Ningbo, China; the first Chinese woman and first Chinese citizen educated entirely in mainland China to win a Nobel in science
Discovered artemisinin (qinghaosu) in 1972 from *Artemisia annua* (sweet wormwood), a plant used in Chinese medicine for ~2,000 years
Artemisinin-based combination therapies are the WHO's first-line treatment for malaria, saving millions of lives
Found the lead in ancient Chinese texts (Su Honggjing, 340 CE); her work was initially secret under China's 'Project 523'
Sometimes called 'the professor of three nos': no doctoral degree, no postdoctoral research, and no membership in the Chinese Academy of Sciences when she made the discovery

items.prompt: Which British theoretical physicist won the 2013 Nobel Prize in Physics, along with François Englert, for predicting a fundamental particle that gives other particles their mass, confirmed at CERN in 2012?
items.explanation:
Nobel Prize in Physics 2013, shared with François Englert (Robert Brout, who co-developed the theory with Englert, died in 2011)
Predicted the Higgs boson in 1964; confirmed by CERN's Large Hadron Collider on July 4, 2012
The Higgs field permeates all space; particles acquire mass by interacting with it; the Higgs boson is the field's carrier particle
Popularly called the 'God particle' — a term Higgs himself disliked intensely
The discovery validated the Standard Model of particle physics, completing predictions for 17 fundamental particles

items.prompt: Which two scientists won the 2020 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for developing CRISPR-Cas9, a precise gene-editing tool described as 'genetic scissors' that has transformed biology and medicine?
items.explanation:
Nobel Prize in Chemistry 2020 — the first time the Chemistry prize was awarded exclusively to two women
CRISPR (Clustered Regularly Interspaced Short Palindromic Repeats): a bacterial immune defense mechanism repurposed as a programmable DNA editing tool
Their landmark *Science* paper (June 2012) showed CRISPR-Cas9 could precisely cut any targeted DNA sequence
Applications: sickle cell disease treatment (first approved CRISPR therapy, 2023), agricultural biotechnology, and cancer research
Faced a complex patent dispute with the Broad Institute (Feng Zhang) over rights to CRISPR in human cells

items.prompt: Which two scientists won the 2023 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine for discoveries about mRNA nucleoside modifications that enabled the development of COVID-19 mRNA vaccines?
items.explanation:
Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine 2023 for showing that modified nucleosides in synthetic mRNA suppress the immune response that would otherwise destroy it — making mRNA therapeutics viable
Karikó spent decades pursuing mRNA therapy while being repeatedly demoted and losing grants due to lack of institutional support
Their key 2005 paper was initially rejected by several journals
Directly enabled BioNTech/Pfizer and Moderna COVID-19 vaccines; over 13 billion doses administered globally by 2023
Karikó is only the 13th woman to win the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine

items.prompt: Which American physicist is the only person to have won the Nobel Prize in Physics twice — in 1956 for co-inventing the transistor and in 1972 for the BCS theory of superconductivity?
items.explanation:
Nobel Prize in Physics 1956 (shared with Walter Brattain and William Shockley) for inventing the transistor at Bell Labs on December 23, 1947
Nobel Prize in Physics 1972 (shared with Leon Cooper and John Robert Schrieffer) for BCS theory explaining superconductivity at the atomic level
The only person to win the Nobel Prize in Physics twice
After Shockley excluded Bardeen and Brattain from further transistor research, Bardeen left Bell Labs for the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign
The transistor underpins all modern electronics; Bardeen's double Nobel uniquely spans both its invention and a second major branch of physics
items.cardFront: Only person to win Nobel Physics twice

items.prompt: Which Spanish pathologist, using silver staining to map the nervous system, proved it consists of discrete individual neurons rather than a continuous network — sharing the Nobel Prize in Medicine in 1906 with his chief opponent?
items.explanation:
Shared Nobel Medicine 1906 with Camillo Golgi — who used his own Nobel lecture to argue against the neuron doctrine that Cajal had proved
Established the neuron doctrine: the nervous system is composed of discrete individual cells, not a continuous reticulum
Created extraordinarily detailed hand drawings of neural structures, still reproduced in neuroscience textbooks today
Used Golgi’s own silver-staining technique to refute Golgi’s reticular theory — one of science’s great ironies
Called the ‘father of modern neuroscience’; born 1852 in Petilla de Aragón, Spain; died 1934
items.cardFront: Medicine, 1906 — neuron doctrine, father of neuroscience

items.prompt: Which German physician identified the bacteria causing tuberculosis (1882) and cholera (1883), established the four postulates for proving a microorganism causes a disease, and won the Nobel Prize in Medicine in 1905?
items.explanation:
Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine 1905 for tuberculosis research
Identified *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* in 1882 — TB killed roughly 1 in 7 people in Europe at the time
Koch’s Postulates (1884): four criteria establishing that a specific microbe causes a specific disease; still foundational in infectious disease research
Identified the cholera bacillus *Vibrio cholerae* in 1883 during a field investigation in Egypt and India
Rivalry with Louis Pasteur was intense and often nationalistic (Franco-Prussian War era); called the ‘father of bacteriology’

items.prompt: Which British biochemist is the only person to win the Nobel Prize in Chemistry twice — in 1958 for sequencing insulin and in 1980 for developing the DNA chain-termination sequencing method?
items.explanation:
Nobel Chemistry 1958: determined the complete amino acid sequence of insulin — the first protein ever fully sequenced; established that proteins have a definite sequence
Nobel Chemistry 1980 (shared with Walter Gilbert and Paul Berg): developed the Sanger dideoxy chain-termination method for reading DNA sequences
Sanger sequencing became the workhorse of the Human Genome Project (1990–2003)
Only person to win the Nobel Prize in Chemistry twice (Marie Curie won Chemistry and Peace; John Bardeen won Physics twice)
Declined a knighthood, preferring to be called ‘Fred’; retired at 65 to tend his garden

items.prompt: Which Swedish paleogeneticist sequenced the Neanderthal genome (2010) and discovered the Denisovans from a single finger bone — founding the field of ancient DNA research and winning the Nobel Prize in Medicine in 2022?
items.explanation:
Nobel Physiology or Medicine 2022, sole laureate; founded the field of paleogenetics
Neanderthal genome (2010): revealed that non-African modern humans carry 1–4% Neanderthal DNA, proving interbreeding occurred
Denisovans discovered entirely from a 40,000-year-old finger bone from Denisova Cave, Siberia — an entirely new human species identified through DNA alone
Director of the Max Planck Institute for Evolutionary Anthropology in Leipzig
Son of Nobel laureate Sune Bergström (Medicine 1982) — making them the only father-son pair to both win the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine

items.prompt: Which two scientists won the 2024 Nobel Prize in Physics for foundational work enabling machine learning with artificial neural networks — surprising many by awarding a Physics prize to computer science research?
items.explanation:
Nobel Prize in Physics 2024, awarded to John J. Hopfield and Geoffrey E. Hinton
Hopfield created the Hopfield network (1982): a recurrent neural network that stores and recalls patterns, inspired by the physics of spin glasses
Hinton developed the Boltzmann machine and backpropagation, enabling practical training of deep neural networks; his 2012 team's AlexNet triggered the deep learning revolution
Hinton left Google in 2023 to speak freely about AI safety risks; called 'the godfather of AI'
The Nobel committee justified the award as statistical physics methods applied to neural network systems; many physicists questioned the classification

items.prompt: Which two American biologists won the 2024 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine for discovering microRNA, tiny RNA molecules that regulate gene expression in all multicellular organisms?
items.explanation:
Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine 2024 for discovering microRNA and its role in post-transcriptional gene regulation
Working with the nematode *C. elegans*, discovered that small RNA molecules (lin-4, let-7) regulate developmental timing by binding to mRNA and blocking translation
microRNAs are ~22 nucleotides long; humans have over 1,000 types regulating ~60% of protein-coding genes
Dysregulation of microRNAs is linked to cancer, cardiovascular disease, and neurological disorders — they are active targets for drug therapy
The discovery added a major new layer of gene regulation beyond the Central Dogma's DNA → RNA → protein framework
